questions.question: Explain the procedure for mapping a 1:1 relationship type from ER diagram to relational schema. Include the foreign key approach in your answer.
keyPoints: Choose one entity as the referencing entity
Add the primary key of the other entity as foreign key
Include the attributes of the relationship in the referencing table
Alternative: merge both entities into a single relation if appropriate

questions.question: Describe how multi-valued attributes and derived attributes are handled differently in ER-to-relational mapping. Provide reasoning for each case.
keyPoints: Multi-valued: create a separate relation with foreign key back to the entity
Derived attributes: do not store in the relation, compute when needed
Multi-valued needs its own primary key (composite)
Derived attributes save storage and prevent update anomalies

questions.question: Consider an ER diagram with entities Student (StudentID, Name, Age) and Course (CourseID, Title, Credits). A student can enroll in many courses, and each course can have many students (N:M relationship Enrollment with attribute Grade). Also, each course is taught by one professor (1:N relationship Teaches with entity Professor having attributes ProfID and Department). Explain the complete relational mapping process, showing all resulting relations with their primary keys and foreign keys.
keyPoints: Student maps to Student(StudentID, Name, Age)
Course maps to Course(CourseID, Title, Credits)
N:M Enrollment creates Enrollment(StudentID, CourseID, Grade) with composite primary key
1:N Teaches adds CourseID as foreign key in Professor or creates Teaches(CourseID, ProfID)

questions.question: Explain how to map recursive (unary) relationships in ER diagrams. Also, explain how total participation constraint affects the relational mapping with an example involving an entity Employee with a recursive relationship Manages (where each employee is managed by another employee, and the CEO is not managed by anyone).
keyPoints: Recursive relationships use the same entity's primary key as foreign key
Add a foreign key attribute (e.g., ManagerID) to the entity relation
For total participation, either enforce NOT NULL or use separate relation
CEO has NULL for ManagerID; other employees reference their manager's EmployeeID
